## Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

## Forensic Science International: Digital Investigation

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/fsidi

## Large language models in digital forensics: capabilities, challenges and future directions

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Maxim Chernyshev ∗ iD , Zubair Baig, Naeem Syed, Robin Doss, Malcolm Shore

Deakin Cyber Research and Innovation Centre (Deakin Cyber), Deakin University, 75 Pigdons Rd, Waurn Ponds, 3216, Victoria, Australia

## H I G H L I G H T S

- Systematic review of 33 works mapping LLM capabilities to DFRWS forensic phases.
- LLMs achieve 85%-98% accuracy across diverse forensic tasks with limitations.
- Probabilistic LLM outputs conflict with deterministic forensic requirements.
- Three strategic integration points identified for LLM-assisted investigations.

## A R T I C L E   I N F O

Keywords: Generative artificial intelligence Large language models LLM Digital forensics

## 1  .  Introduction

The field  of  digital  forensics  has  continuously  evolved  to  address the  challenges  posed  by  emerging  technologies,  from  the  prolifera­ tion of personal computers and mobile devices to the advent of cloud computing and the Internet of Things (IoT). Each technological shift has necessitated the development of new investigative methodologies, tools,  and  frameworks  to  effectively  identify,  preserve,  collect,  ex­ amine, and analyse digital evidence. The most recent transformation in  this  landscape  stems  from  the  rapid  advancement and widespread adoption of generative artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large lan­ guage  models  (LLMs)  such  as ChatGPT , GPT-4 , Claude ,  and Gemini .

## A B S T R A C T

The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has simultaneously created opportunities and challenges for digital forensic science. This survey systematically examines the emerging intersection between generative artificial intelligence and digital forensics through our analysis of 33 peer-reviewed works. We map LLM capa­ bilities  across the established Digital Forensic Research Workshop (DFRWS) process model, identifying three strategic  integration  points  where  these  technologies  demonstrate  measurable  benefits  -  pattern  recognition during  the  examination  phase,  evidence  analysis  during  the  analysis  phase,  and  evidence  presentation  and reporting during the presentation phase. Our findings show that LLMs achieve substantial performance improve­ ments across diverse forensic tasks, but critical challenges persist, including the fundamental tension between the  probabilistic  nature  of  LLM  outputs  and  deterministic  forensic  requirements,  alongside  concerns  regard­ ing explainability, reproducibility, and legal admissibility. We identify significant research gaps in validation frameworks, forensic-ready architectures, and standardised evaluation protocols. The survey establishes a com­ prehensive research agenda spanning technical, methodological, and legal domains, emphasising the necessity for interdisciplinary collaboration and human-AI collaborative approaches to preserve forensic integrity when leveraging LLM capabilities.

These sophisticated AI systems, trained on vast corpora of text data, demonstrate remarkable capabilities in natural language understanding, pattern recognition, and automated reasoning that closely align with the core analytical requirements in digital forensic investigations (Scanlon et al., 2023a). The emergence of LLMs presents an opportunity for the forensic community to explore how these powerful AI capabilities can enhance  traditional  forensic  workflows  to  address  critical  scalability challenges facing contemporary digital forensic science (Du et al., 2020; Casino et al., 2022).

However, the integration of LLMs into digital forensic practice intro­ duces fundamental tensions between the capabilities these models offer

∗ Corresponding author. Email address: mchernyshev@deakin.edu.au (M. Chernyshev).

Received 26 October 2025; Received in revised form 30 December 2025; Accepted 30 December 2025

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

and the rigour of digital forensic science. Existing digital forensic tools and methodologies, which have been designed for deterministic systems with  predictable  evidence  patterns,  face  significant  challenges  when confronted with probabilistic outputs and non-deterministic behaviour of LLM-based systems (Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024). The foun­ dational principles of digital forensics, specifically reproducibility, chain of custody maintenance, and legal admissibility, must be carefully recon­ sidered when incorporating AI-assisted capabilities that may generate varying outputs across multiple invocations of identical inputs such as those generated by LLMs. This convergence of opportunities and chal­ lenges establishes a critical research gap at the intersection of AI systems and digital forensic science, where existing methodologies must evolve to accommodate the application of LLM capabilities to enhance forensic workflows.

This survey provides a comprehensive examination of the emerging intersection between generative AI, specifically LLMs, and digital foren­ sics, synthesising the rapidly evolving body of research that has emerged since the mainstream adoption of ChatGPT in late 2022. Based on a sys­ tematic literature review of 33 peer-reviewed works published between 2023  and  2025,  we  map  the  current  landscape  of  LLM  applications across  the  established  Digital  Forensic  Research  Workshop  (DFRWS) process model (Palmer, 2001), identifying both promising capabilities and critical limitations.

Our analysis demonstrates a clear progression from exploratory in­ vestigations,  to  methodological  developments  and  growing  empirical validation efforts, with research efforts predominantly concentrated in the examination and analysis phases of digital forensic investigations. We identify significant gaps in current research such as limited atten­ tion to validation controls necessary for maintaining forensic integrity when using AI-assisted methods, and insufficient exploration of auto­ mated structured evidence representation using established ontological frameworks. This survey makes three primary contributions:

1. A systematic mapping of LLM capabilities to forensic processes based on the foundational DFRWS process model, identifying in­ tegration points where these technologies can provide measurable benefits whilst highlighting areas where human expertise remains essential.
2. Synthesis of key challenges that must be addressed for the prac­ tical deployment of LLM-assisted forensic methods in operational digital forensic contexts.
3. A  comprehensive  research  agenda  that  identifies  priority  areas for  future  investigation,  including  the  development  of  forensicready LLM architectures, standardised validation frameworks, and interdisciplinary approaches.

The remainder of this survey is organised as follows. Section 2 es­ tablishes  the  foundational  concepts  necessary  for  understanding  the intersection of LLMs and digital forensics, including the DFRWS process model, LLM architectures and capabilities, and the unique character­ istics  of  LLM-generated  evidence.  Section  3  describes  our  systematic literature  review  methodology  encompassing  the  search  strategy  and the associated inclusion criteria, as well as the analytical approach used to identify and synthesise relevant research based on published stateof-the-art. Section 4 examines LLM-assisted forensic capabilities across the DFRWS process model, highlighting demonstrated capabilities and their inherent limitations through systematic mapping of the included literature. Section 5 provides a comprehensive analysis of the techni­ cal,  methodological,  and  other  challenges  facing  LLM-assisted  digital forensics, including issues of explainability, reproducibility, and forensic integrity. Section 6 outlines future research directions across technical, methodological, and legal domains, establishing a comprehensive re­ search agenda for advancing the intersection of generative AI and digital forensics. Finally, Section 7 discusses the implications of our findings for both research and practice, and Section 8 concludes with a synthesis of key contributions and a call to action for interdisciplinary collaboration in this rapidly evolving domain.

## 2  .  Background and terminology

This section introduces the core concepts, terminologies, and frame­ works  that  underpin  subsequent  discussions  on  LLM  integration  into forensic workflows.

## 2.1  .  Digital forensics fundamentals

Digital forensics encompasses the scientifically derived and validated methods  used  to  identify,  preserve,  collect,  analyse,  interpret,  docu­ ment, and present digital evidence derived from digital sources (Palmer, 2001). The field has evolved significantly from its traditional origins in computer forensics to encompass diverse technological domains in­ cluding mobile devices, cloud computing, and Internet of Things (IoT) systems (Al-Dhaqm et al., 2021). Understanding the foundational princi­ ples and processes of digital forensics is essential for examining how LLM capabilities can be integrated into investigative workflows alongside the required scientific rigour.

## 2.1.1  .  The DFRWS process model

The Digital Forensic Research Workshop (DFRWS) process model  (Palmer,  2001),  shown  in  Fig.  1,  established  the  founda­ tional  framework for conducting digital investigations, encompassing six  core  phases  that  guide  forensic  practitioners  through  systematic evidence-handling workflows. The identification phase involves recog­ nising  potential  sources  of  digital  evidence  across  diverse  electronic devices and systems, from traditional computers to contemporary IoT devices and cloud infrastructure. The preservation phase ensures the in­ tegrity of identified evidence through approaches such as cryptographic hashing and appropriate storage mechanisms, maintaining an unbroken chain of custody essential for subsequent evidential admissibility. The collection phase  focuses  on  the  acquisition  of  digital  artefacts  using forensically sound methods, typically involving write-blocking devices and validated forensic tools to create bit-for-bit copies of storage media. The examination phase entails systematic assessment and extraction of relevant  information  from  collected  data,  often  requiring  specialised techniques to circumvent encryption, recover deleted files, and process large volumes of heterogeneous data. The analysis phase represents the interpretive element of digital forensic investigations, where practition­ ers  may  need  to  correlate  evidence  across  multiple  sources,  develop and  test  hypotheses,  and  subsequently  establish  causal  relationships between  digital  artefacts  and  investigative  questions  (Casey,  2011). Finally,  the presentation phase  involves  documenting  findings  and providing expert testimony.

Fig. 1. The phases of the digital forensic research workshop (DFRWS) process model. Adapted from (Palmer, 2001).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## 2.1.2  .  Evolution of digital evidence sources

Digital evidence sources have evolved from the early focus on per­ sonal  computer  file  systems  to  encompass  increasingly  complex  and distributed technological ecosystems. Traditional evidence sources in­ cluding  hard  drives,  memory  dumps,  and  network  logs  have  been supplemented by cloud-based storage, mobile device artefacts, and IoT sensor data (Khan et al., 2016). The emergence of LLMs and LLM agents represents the latest technological shift in this evolution, introducing fundamentally  new  categories  of  digital  evidence  that  differ  qualita­ tively from traditional artefacts. LLM-integrated systems generate evi­ dence across multiple architectural layers, including conversational logs capturing natural language interactions, agent execution traces docu­ menting autonomous decision-making processes, tool invocation records showing external API calls, and reasoning chains that capture interme­ diate steps in multi-stage processing (Wang et al., 2024). These evidence types also present unique challenges for traditional forensic methodolo­ gies,  as  they  combine  structured  metadata  with  unstructured  natural language content, require an understanding of conversational flow, and may span multiple system boundaries in distributed architectures.

## 2.2  .  Generative AI and large language models

This  subsection  examines  the  evolution  from  traditional  AI  ap­ proaches to contemporary LLMs, establishing the baseline foundation necessary  for  understanding  both  their  forensic  applications  and  the unique challenges they introduce.

## 2.2.1  .  From Artificial Intelligence to Generative AI

The field of AI encompasses computational systems designed to per­ form tasks that typically require human intelligence - such as reasoning, learning, and problem-solving. Within the broader domain of AI, ma­ chine learning (ML) represents a subset of AI techniques that enable such systems to learn patterns from data in the context of specific tasks, while deep learning, as a subset of ML, specialises in neural network architec­ tures with multiple layers that can automatically extract hierarchical features from raw data (Brown et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 2, genera­ tive AI represents a distinct category within deep learning, characterised by models capable of generating content including text, images, audio, and code based on learned patterns from training data. Unlike discrimi­ native ML models that classify or predict based on input data, generative models learn the underlying probability distributions of their training data  to  produce  new  instances  resembling  the  original  corpus  (Shao et al., 2024).

Fig. 2. The relationship between artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), deep learning, generative AI and large language models (LLMs).

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

LLMs  constitute  a  prominent  class  of  generative  AI  systems  built upon  transformer  architectures  (Vaswani  et  al.,  2017).  Transformer LLMs process and generate human-like text through self-attention mech­ anisms and are trained on vast corpora comprising billions of tokens. LLMs  demonstrate  emergent  capabilities  in  natural  language  under­ standing, reasoning, and task generalisation that distinguish them from earlier  natural  language  processing  approaches.  Thus,  contemporary LLMs such as GPT , Claude , and Gemini families carry sophisticated abili­ ties in contextual comprehension and multi-step reasoning - capabilities that align closely with the analytical requirements of digital forensic investigations (Scanlon et al., 2023a).

## 2.2.2  .  LLM architectures and forensically relevant capabilities

The  transformer  architecture  behind  modern  LLMs  relies  on  selfattention  mechanisms  that  enable  parallel  processing  of  entire  text sequences, thereby allowing models to capture long-range dependen­ cies and contextual relationships required for understanding complex narratives (Vaswani et al., 2017). This architectural foundation supports several capabilities directly relevant to digital forensic applications:

1. Contextual understanding allows LLMs to interpret ambiguous refer­ ences, resolve pronouns, and maintain coherence across extended conversational  exchanges  (Nair  et  al.,  2020),  which  can  prove valuable when analysing communication logs and reconstructing event sequences from fragmented evidence.
2. Pattern recognition capabilities allow LLMs to identify anomalous behaviours and attack signatures, as well as correlate disparate evidence sources without requiring explicit rule-based program­ ming (Lin, 2024).
3. Semantic reasoning allows models to infer implicit relationships be­ tween entities and generate hypotheses about causal connections, alongside explanations and reasoning that could support investiga­ tive decision-making (Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024).

However, these capabilities come with inherent limitations that di­ rectly impact digital forensic applications. The probabilistic nature of LLM output generation, controlled by parameters such as temperature, introduces non-determinism where identical inputs may produce vary­ ing  outputs  across  multiple  invocations  (Ouyang  et  al.,  2022).  This characteristic fundamentally conflicts with the reproducibility require­ ments  essential  for  digital  forensic  method  acceptance  and  the  legal admissibility  of  digital  evidence.  Furthermore,  LLMs  demonstrate  a strong susceptibility to hallucinations (Zhang et al., 2025), generating incredibly plausible, yet factually incorrect information that can mislead investigations if not properly validated. Finally, the context window lim­ itations inherent to transformer architectures also potentially constrain the volume of information that can be processed simultaneously, with contemporary models ranging from thousands to millions of tokens, po­ tentially fragmenting the analysis of large-scale forensic datasets (Liu et al., 2024).

## 3  .  Systematic literature review methodology

Having established the foundational concepts spanning digital foren­ sics and LLM technologies, we now turn to examine the current state of research at this intersection.

## 3.1  .  Review protocol

This  systematic  literature  review  follows  the  Preferred  Reporting Items  for  Systematic  Reviews  and  Meta-Analyses  (PRISMA)  guide­ lines (Moher et al., 2010) to develop comprehensive and reproducible coverage  of  research  at  the  intersection  of  generative  AI  and  digital forensics. The review protocol includes four key components - search strategy,  inclusion  and  exclusion  criteria,  workflow  adaptation,  and quality assessment.

The search  strategy employed  ego  graph  analysis  to  identify  rele­ vant search terms across two conceptual domains. For generative AI, the ego graph centred on 'generative AI' and produced terms includ­ ing LLM , large language model , GPT , and ChatGPT . For digital forensics, the ego graph centred on 'digital forensics' and yielded terms includ­ ing computer forensics and cyber forensics . These terms were combined using Boolean operators to construct database-specific queries targeting four major research databases Scopus , Web of Science , IEEE Xplore , and ScienceDirect . The search results were limited to items published by October 31, 2025, to capture the most recent developments in this rapidly evolving field.

Inclusion criteria required that records: (1) contain at least one search term from each conceptual domain in the title, abstract, or keywords; (2) be published between 2023 and 2025; (3) be associated with computer science, engineering, or social sciences subject areas where such filter­ ing was supported by the database; (4) be written in English; and (5) represent peer-reviewed journal articles or conference papers. Exclusion criteria eliminated records lacking essential metadata (title, year, source, abstract, DOI), non-English publications, grey literature, and works not addressing LLM applications in digital forensics.  As  shown  in  Fig.  3, the initial database queries returned 248 records, which were reduced to 33 included works following deduplication, screening, and full-text eligibility assessment.

The PRISMA workflow adaptation involved three sequential filtering stages. First, automated deduplication removed identical records based on title, year, and DOI matching. Second, abstract screening applied the inclusion criteria to eliminate irrelevant works, with particular atten­ tion to identifying studies addressing LLM-assisted forensic analysis or closely related domains such as cyber incident response. Third, full-text eligibility  assessment  examined  the  remaining  works  to  confirm  sub­ stantive contributions to the intersection of generative AI and digital forensics, with particular focus on methodological rigour and empirical validation where applicable.

Finally, quality  assessment employed  natural  language  processing techniques, including stopword removal and Porter stemming (Willett, 2006), to facilitate automated analysis of abstracts and full texts. Each included work was systematically mapped to the DFRWS process model phases to assess coverage across the forensic workflow, with particular attention to methodological contributions, empirical validation status, and model architectures explored.

Fig.  3. PRISMA (Preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and metaanalyses)  flowchart  of  study  inclusions  and  exclusions  for  the  systematic literature review.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig.  4. Top  20  bigrams  from  abstract  (a)  and  included  full-text  record  (b) analysis.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## 3.2  .  Literature landscape analysis

Bigramfrequency  analysis  of  the  included  literature,  as  shown in  Fig.  4,  identifies  the  conceptual  landscape  spanning  LLM-assisted digital  forensics.  Abstract  analysis  (Fig.  4(a))  demonstrates  that dig­ ital  forensics (65  occurrences)  and large  language (59  occurrences) represent  the  most  prominent  themes,  followed  by language  models (46  occurrences), deep  learning (40  occurrences),  and digital  foren­ sic (40  occurrences).  Notably,  domain-specific  applications  emerge through terms such as deepfake detection (23 occurrences), threat intel­ ligence (18 occurrences), and cyber threats (18 occurrences), alongside law  enforcement (18  occurrences),  highlighting  practical  investigative contexts.

Full-text analysis (Fig. 4(b)) further shows substantially higher fre­ quency counts, with digital forensics appearing 306 times, large language 176 times, and language  models 150 times. The detailed examination also  identifies  specific  methodological  concepts,  including  fi ne-tuning (102 occurrences), timeline analysis (99 occurrences), threat hunting (107 occurrences), and zero-shot (56 occurrences), which demonstrate a di­ verse range of technical approaches explored across the literature. The prominence of natural language (86 occurrences) and entity recognition (53  occurrences)  underscores  the  focus  on  natural  language  process­ ing capabilities inherent to LLMs. Furthermore, digital investigation (59 occurrences) and forensic investigations (63 occurrences) collectively em­ phasise  the  investigative  methodology  focus,  whereas knowledge  base (67 occurrences) and LLM-based (58 occurrences) indicate growing at­ tention to knowledge-driven LLM-assisted approaches. The substantial frequency  of real-world (56  occurrences)  suggests  increasing  empha­ sis  on  practical  deployment  considerations  and  empirical  validation requirements.

The temporal evolution of included literature demonstrates a clear progression from foundational explorations to empirical validation ef­ forts across the analysed literature period. The year 2023 marked the emergence of exploratory research following the mainstream adoption of ChatGPT in  late  2022,  with  foundational  works  such  as  those  by Scanlon  et  al.  (2023a); Henseler  and  van  Beek  (2023); Sreya  et  al. (2023) establishing conceptual frameworks and conducting initial capa­ bility assessments. These early contributions predominantly employed qualitative methodologies and manual experimentation approaches, to assess  the  potential  and  probe  for  any  critical  limitations  of  LLM  in­ tegration  in  digital  forensic  contexts.  Subsequent  works  incorporated significant methodological maturation, characterised by increased quan­ titative  evaluation  based  on  controlled  experimental  designs,  as  well as  domain-specific  specialisation,  such  as  works  by  Lin  (2024); Oh et  al.  (2024); Wickramasekara  and  Scanlon  (2024).  This  period  in­ troduced multi-agent frameworks, zero-shot learning approaches, and hybrid AI architectures that demonstrated growing technical sophistica­ tion. Finally, the most recent literature, such as studies by Sharma et al. (2025); Khatiwala et al. (2025); Kim et al. (2025b), represents a culmina­ tion of earlier developments focusing on rigorous empirical validation, purpose-built forensic LLMs, and real-world deployment considerations based on data from real criminal investigations.

The systematic mapping of included works to the DFRWS process model phases, as presented in Table 1, highlights a significant concen­ tration of research efforts within the examination and analysis phases, with the vast majority of the 33 included works addressing these core investigative  activities  to  some  degree.  This  distribution  reflects  the natural alignment between LLM capabilities in pattern recognition and automated reasoning with the information processing requirements in­ herent  to  post-collection  digital  forensic  activities.  The identification phase receives attention from only eight works (24%), specifically those proposing comprehensive end-to-end frameworks (Sreya et al., 2023; Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024; Khatiwala et al., 2025; Parsonage and Dempsey, 2025), ontology-based approaches for guiding artefact identification  (Zhang  and  Xie,  2024),  or  holistic  investigative  work­ flows (Shang et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2025). The presentation phase is addressed by 23 works (69%) showing substantial focus on automated report generation and evidence communication capabilities, while the preservation and collection phases receive minimal coverage. This gap likely reflects the technical nature of these phases, which traditionally rely  on  specialised  hardware  and  software  tools  rather  than  natural language  capabilities  where  LLMs  could  offer  potential  comparative advantages.

- not covered), with associated listings of large The diversity of LLM architectures explored across the included liter­ ature, as documented in Table 1, demonstrates the breadth of available models considered by researchers. Proprietary models from OpenAI, in­ cluding various iterations of GPT , dominate the landscape, followed by Google's Gemini family and Anthropic's Claude models. Open-weight alternatives,  such  as  Meta's LLaMA family,  also  represent  a  growing trend  towards  privacy-preserving  local  deployment  options  that  can be adopted to address data confidentiality concerns inherent in cloudbased forensic  analysis  using  third-party  services.  Specialised  models such as BERT and RoBERTa appear in limited knowledge graph applica­ tions (Zhang and Xie, 2024), while domain-specific fine-tuned variants like PassGPT (Imhof et al., 2024) and ForensicLLM (Sharma et al., 2025) demonstrate emerging recognition of the value of purpose-built digi­ tal  forensic LLMs. Notably, only a few works explore multiple model architectures comparatively.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

The expanding empirical validation landscape showcases the ongo­ ing maturation of this research area, with 25 works (75%) including experimental  results  or  empirical  evidence  to  support  proposed  ap­ proaches.  This  trend  represents  a  significant  shift  from  the  focus  on theoretical contributions in early exploratory works towards systematic evaluation  protocols  incorporating  quantitative  performance  metrics, controlled experimental designs, and real-world dataset utilisation. The progression  from  proof-of-concept  demonstrations  in  2023  to  rigor­ ous  validation  frameworks  in  2025  demonstrates  the  field's  ongoing evolution towards operational readiness.

However, several critical  research  gaps  also  emerge  from  this  lit­ erature  landscape.  While  theoretical  frameworks  for  LLM  integration have been proposed (Sreya et al., 2023; Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024),  empirical  validation  of  comprehensive  process  model  exten­ sions with appropriate validation controls remains absent. Additionally, automated generation of structured forensic evidence representations using established ontological frameworks has received limited attention, with existing knowledge graph approaches confined to specific digital forensic domains such as IoT (Zhang and Xie, 2024), rather than compre­ hensive forensic entity extraction and relationship mapping. Finally, the predominant reliance on synthetic datasets, inconsistent evaluation pro­ tocols, and insufficient attention to forensic integrity requirements high­ light fundamental challenges that must be addressed for the practical deployment of LLM-assisted forensic methods in operational contexts.

## 4  .  LLM-assisted forensic capabilities

The  integration  of  LLM  capabilities  into  digital  forensic  work­ flows requires systematic mapping to established investigative processes to  identify  where  these  technologies  can  provide  validated  bene­ fits.  Building upon the foundational DFRWS process model presented in Section 2, our analysis of the included literature identifies three strate­ gic integration points that align LLM capabilities with specific forensic requirements pattern recognition during the examination phase, evidence analysis during the analysis phase, and evidence presentation and reporting during the presentation phase. As illustrated in Fig. 5, these integration points reflect the current state of research, which shows concentrated development in the examination, analysis, and presentation phases of the DFRWS model.

Notably, the identification, preservation, and collection phases re­ main largely  unaddressed  in  current  literature  with  the  exception  of inclusion  in  non-empirical  works  proposing  holistic  frameworks  such as  those  by    Sreya  et  al.  (2023); Khatiwala  et  al.  (2025); Parsonage and Dempsey (2025). This distribution reflects the natural alignment be­ tween LLM capabilities in natural language processing and automated reasoning  with  the  information  processing  requirements  inherent  to post-collection digital forensic activities, whilst the technical nature of early-phase  activities  that  rely  on  specialised  hardware  and  software tools presents fewer natural opportunities for LLM integration.

## 4.1  .  Pattern recognition

Pattern recognition represents a critical integration point during the examination phase, where LLMs assist investigators in identifying foren­ sic  artefacts,  detecting  anomalous behaviours, and recognising attack signatures  across  heterogeneous  data  sources    (Lin,  2024).  The  abil­ ity  of  LLMs to process unstructured data, recognise complex patterns without explicit rule-based programming, and adapt to novel evidence types through zero-shot or few-shot learning approaches presents sig­ nificant potential for addressing scalability challenges in digital forensic examination.

## 4.1.1  .  Artefact identification and source recognition

During the  examination  of  collected  evidence,  LLMs  can  assist  in identifying  relevant  artefacts  across  diverse  digital  evidence  types. Scanlon et al. (2023a) conducted a systematic evaluation of ChatGPT across  multiple  forensic  contexts,  showing  that  the  model  could  ef­ fectively identify operating system artefacts, cloud storage interaction evidence,  and  file  execution  traces  when  provided  with  appropriate contextual information. Their experiments demonstrated that ChatGPT could specify relevant artefacts including Windows Event Logs, registry hives, and prefetch files; however the model exhibited variable depth in responses across different artefact categories. Furthermore, the model showed capability in identifying cloud platform-specific artefacts, in­ cluding Google Drive desktop application paths and configuration files, albeit with occasional inaccuracies in path resolution.

## 4.1.2  .  Anomaly and suspicious activity detection

Agus  Ariesta  Putra  et  al.  (2024)  developed  a  zero-shot  learning approach  for  anomaly  detection  in  forensic  timelines,  evaluating  six pre-trained models across five public datasets including Digital Corpora and  Honeynet  Challenge  scenarios.  The  associated  methodology  em­ ployed sentiment analysis as a proxy for anomaly identification, treating negative  sentiment  classifications  as  indicators  of  suspicious  events, where models such as sileod-deberta demonstrated that zero-shot ap­ proaches can effectively identify anomalous log entries without domainspecific training. However, the study also emphasised significant per­ formance variability across models, highlighting the critical importance of model selection for operational deployment. The authors also iden­ tified that model performance degraded substantially when processing non-descriptive log entries, emphasising the continued importance of log quality and natural language context for effective LLM-assisted analysis.

Loumachi et al. (2025) extended anomaly detection to cyber inci­ dent timeline analysis through their GenDFIR framework, which inte­ grates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Llama 3.1 8B for semantic timeline enrichment. The framework achieved an overall per­ formance rate of 97.51% across multiple evaluation metrics, showing superior capability not only in identifying anomalous events, but also in providing contextual interpretation of their significance within inci­ dent sequences. The GenDFIR approach addresses a critical limitation of traditional anomaly detection by incorporating attention-based mecha­ nisms that weigh the relevance of each event relative to investigative queries,  allowing  for  more  nuanced  identification  of  suspicious  pat­ terns. This framework represents a significant advancement over simple sentiment-based approaches, achieving 100% accuracy in evidence re­ trieval tasks across six diverse incident scenarios including SYN flood attacks, phishing campaigns, and DNS spoofing incidents.

## 4.1.3  .  Pattern generation and matching

Scanlon et al. (2023a) explored using ChatGPT to generate regular expressions and keyword lists to support digital forensic investigations. When prompted to create regular expressions for common entities such as credit card numbers, the model produced detailed patterns with expla­ nations of constituent parts, including specific starting digits for various card  providers.  However,  the  generated  expressions  exhibited  limita­ tions, such as failing to account for whitespace between number groups. Similarly, when generating regular expressions for UK car registration plates, ChatGPT successfully created accurate patterns for the post-2001 scheme but included disclaimers about potential false positives due to unvalidated area code combinations. Overall, the quality of generated regular expressions and keyword lists requires validation through test­ ing, as demonstrated by instances where provided test examples failed to match the generated patterns.

## 4.1.4  .  Log anomaly detection with RAG

Pan  et  al.  (2024)  introduced RAG-Log ,  a  framework  that  inte­ grates retrieval-augmented generation with GPT-3.5 for automated log anomaly detection. The framework addresses the challenge of process­ ing large-scale log datasets by combining RAG with LLM capabilities to identify suspicious patterns and anomalous entries. RAG-Log achieved 94.6% precision and 91.2% recall in identifying suspicious log entries, while reducing analysis time from an average of 4.2 h to 18 min for datasets containing 100,000 log entries. The framework features a vec­ tor database to store normalised log samples and uses semantic similarity search to retrieve relevant historical patterns during anomaly detection. However,  the  approach  carries  critical  dependencies  on  high-quality training data and remains vulnerable to adversarial evasion through log manipulation, representing significant limitations that require further investigation.

## 4.1.5  .  Threat intelligence correlation

Threat intelligence correlation provides the ability to identify and contextualise attack patterns across distributed log sources. Lin (2024) developed Thelma ,  a  threat hunting framework based on Code Llama and LLaMA 3 to automate the correlation of threat indicators across het­ erogeneous security  data  sources.  The  framework  achieved  detection rates  exceeding  85% across various attack patterns by systematically mapping observed indicators to the MITRE ATT&amp;CK framework, demon­ strating the capability of LLMs to perform complex pattern recognition tasks  that  traditionally  required  extensive  manual  analysis.  The  sys­ tem successfully  identified  Indicators  of  Compromise  (IoCs),  mapped threat actor techniques to established taxonomies, and recognised attack patterns across distributed logs through automated correlation of tem­ poral and behavioural indicators. However, the reliance on proprietary datasets limits the reproducibility of the presented approach, and the evaluation methodology lacks standardised benchmarks that would en­ able meaningful comparison with alternative approaches. Despite these limitations, Thelma demonstrates that LLM-based systems can effectively automate the labour-intensive process of threat intelligence correlation with accuracy levels comparable to expert human analysts.

## 4.1.6  .  Threat hunting automation

The previously introduced Thelma framework utilises Code Llama and LLaMA 3 to automate hypothesis prioritisation and threat-hunting query generation, achieving detection rates exceeding 85% across var­ ious  attack  patterns.  This  architecture  addresses  the  labour-intensive nature of traditional threat hunting by automating multi-stage attack pattern recognition and behavioural anomaly detection across system boundaries, with particular effectiveness in automated hypothesis gen­ eration for threat scenarios, where it processes threat intelligence and environmental  context  to  prioritise  potential  threats  systematically. Despite the discussed limitations associated with proprietary dataset us­ age and lack of comparative benchmarks, Thelma represents a significant advancement in automating the 'last-mile delivery' of threat hunting workflows, which traditionally require extensive human expertise and manual intervention.

## 4.2  .  Evidence analysis

Evidence analysis represents the interpretive element of digital foren­ sic investigations, where practitioners correlate evidence across multiple sources, develop and test hypotheses, and establish causal relationships between digital artefacts and investigative questions (Casey, 2011).

## 4.2.1  .  Natural language query formulation

During  the  analysis  of  collected  evidence,  LLMs  can  translate  in­ vestigative  questions  into  structured  queries  based  on  conversational descriptions without requiring deep technical expertise in the associated query languages or data models. Henseler and van Beek (2023) demon­ strated that ChatGPT could effectively translate investigative questions into structured queries for the Hansken forensic platform, which utilises the  Hansken Query Language (HQL). Their experiments showed that after  providing ChatGPT with the HQL manual and trace model doc­ umentation through prompts, the model could generate syntactically correct queries for common forensic scenarios such as identifying emails with attachments within specific date ranges or locating evidence of file  execution  by  particular  users.  However,  the  study  identified  that ChatGPT occasionally forgot to prefix properties with their type identi­ fiers, requiring manual iterative correction through follow-up prompts. Despite these limitations, the approach demonstrated practical utility in reducing the technical barrier for investigators who may lack expertise in specific query languages.

Building upon this foundation, Cho et al. (2025) extended natural language query capabilities to conversational AI platform forensics, de­ veloping a Chrome browser extension that leverages REST API endpoints to retrieve conversation data from Claude .  Their  implementation au­ tomated  the  extraction  of  conversation  histories  by  constructing  API

requests  based  on  natural  language  descriptions  of  the  desired  data, demonstrating  that  LLM-assisted  query  formulation  could  be  applied beyond traditional forensic data sources to emerging generative AI plat­ forms.  The  study  highlighted  that  while  natural  language  interfaces simplified data collection, investigators still required an understanding of the underlying data structures to validate query results and assess the comprehensiveness of gathered evidence.

## 4.2.2  .  Entity recognition in forensic timelines

Forensic  timeline  analysis  requires  extraction  of  meaningful  enti­ ties from temporal event sequences. Talasari et al. (2024) demonstrated the application of zero-shot learning techniques using ChatGPT-3.5 and Claude for automated entity extraction from forensic timeline data gen­ erated by log2timeline plaso , addressing the fundamental challenge where traditional named entity recognition models require extensive la­ beled training data for each entity type, which becomes problematic in  forensic  contexts  where  novel  and  previously  unseen  entities  con­ tinuously emerge across diverse investigative scenarios. The zero-shot approach  showcases  that  LLMs  could  classify  entities  such  as  times­ tamps, log messages, protocols, and service names without requiring domain-specific training data. However, both models exhibited signif­ icant limitations when processing non-descriptive log entries, returning empty strings  for  messages  lacking  sufficient  contextual  information. This finding highlights the continued importance of log quality and de­ scriptiveness in supporting effective automated analysis using LLMs, as performance degraded significantly when confronted with abbreviated or highly technical notations that lacked natural language context and semantics.

## 4.2.3  .  Communication and text analysis

Henseler  and  van  Beek  (2023)  demonstrated ChatGPT 's  capabil­ ity  in  electronic  communication  summarisation  and  message  pattern evaluation,  showing  that  the  model  could  effectively  translate  inves­ tigative questions into structured queries for forensic databases when provided with appropriate documentation and context. Their work high­ lighted the LLM's proficiency in comprehending query languages and data models, allowing investigators to formulate complex searches with­ out requiring extensive technical expertise in specific query syntaxes. However, the study identified limitations when the model lacked con­ textual understanding of specific organisational processes or proprietary data formats, emphasising the importance of domain-specific training re­ quirements for effective forensic applications. Cho et al. (2024) extended this analysis to author profiling tasks, evaluating Korean-language LLMs including Polyglot , EEVE , and Bllossom for age prediction and gender identification from writing style. The study achieved 82.1% accuracy in Korean text classification tasks using the EEVE model, showing that language-specific  model  training  significantly  impacts  forensic  analy­ sis performance. Performance degraded substantially when processing mixed-language content, with accuracy dropping to 58.7%, highlighting limitations  in  multilingual  forensic  scenarios  and  the  need  for  spe­ cialised approaches when analysing cross-linguistic evidence using this approach. Finally, Xu and Deng (2025) proposed hands-on methodolo­ gies based on the use of LLMs to process diverse evidence types whilst maintaining forensic integrity through systematic entity recognition and relationship mapping. The work emphasised the importance of prepro­ cessing strategies and prompt engineering techniques in optimising LLM performance for forensic text analysis tasks, achieving robust entity ex­ traction across multiple mobile applications and data formats including long texts.

## 4.2.4  .  Code analysis and generation

Scanlon et al. (2023a) demonstrated the capability to generate foren­ sic scripts using ChatGPT through systematic evaluation across multiple programming  tasks.  Their  experiments  revealed  that  the  LLM  could produce well-commented Python code for file carving operations, ini­ tially relying on filesystem metadata through pytsk3 before adapting to more sophisticated byte-level carving. The generated scripts successfully implemented header-to-EOF extraction patterns, with some limitations in  handling  file  fragmentation  and  false  positives.  For  RAID  acquisi­ tion scenarios, ChatGPT also showcased proficiency in using Linux tools through Python's subprocess library, generating functions to determine RAID levels via mdadm and create disk images. However, the generated scripts lacked validation mechanisms to verify RAID consistency across multiple  disks  or  confirm  appropriate  disk  counts  for  detected  RAID configurations.

Wickramasekara and Scanlon (2024) proposed a multi-agent frame­ work, where the Coder Agent powered by StarCoder generates forensic scripts based on natural language instructions from the Task Translation Assistant as part of a multi-agent forensic workflow. This architecture demonstrates that LLMs could potentially be used to decompose complex forensic tasks into executable code segments, with predefined skills en­ abling integration with existing forensic platforms through interoperable API layers.

## 4.2.5  .  Multimodal evidence analysis

The integration of vision-capable LLMs represents a significant ad­ vancement in digital forensic capabilities, enabling automated analysis of visual evidence alongside traditional text-based artefacts. Jia et al. (2024) conducted a systematic evaluation of GPT-4V and Gemini 1.0 Pro for detecting AI-generated face images, showing that multimodal LLMs achieved 83.4% and 79.7% accuracy respectively in identifying synthetic imagery through semantic inconsistency detection. However, performance  degraded  substantially  when  analysing  compressed  im­ ages, with accuracy dropping to approximately 62% for images com­ pressed below 50KB, highlighting sensitivity to image quality and postprocessing operations. Similarly, Hawa et al. (2025) explored practical applications of GPT-4V in crime scene analysis, showing that the model could identify evidence items, analyse spatial relationships, and detect visual anomalies in photographic evidence with 88% accuracy compared to expert human analysis. The study showed that vision-capable LLMs reduced average analysis time by 43% whilst maintaining comparable accuracy to human investigators. However, both studies identified criti­ cal limitations including the inability to incorporate signal-level forensic techniques, reliance on semantic rather than statistical cues for element detection, and challenges in handling adversarially manipulated con­ tent. The non-deterministic nature of LLM outputs also poses consistency challenges  requiring  human  oversight  and  validation  in  operational forensic contexts.

## 4.2.6  .  Specialised forensic analysis

Beyond general-purpose  evidence  analysis  capabilities,  contempo­ rary research demonstrates LLM effectiveness across highly specialised forensic domains requiring domain-specific expertise.

Password analysis. Imhof et al. (2024) developed PassGPT , a contextbased password cracking framework that relies on LLM capabilities to generate personalised password dictionaries tailored to specific user de­ mographics and interests. The framework is based on multiple PassGPT models trained on contextual dictionaries derived from leaked password datasets, achieving significant improvements over generic dictionary at­ tacks.  The  presented  experimental  results  showed  that  context-based dictionaries generated via PassGPT successfully cracked passwords that generic approaches failed to identify, with performance improvements of  up  to  50% for passwords deemed difficult to crack by the zxcvbn password strength estimator.  While  the  approach  proved  effective  in generating targeted password candidates, the computational overhead associated with training custom models for each context presents poten­ tial practical deployment challenges in time-sensitive forensic investiga­ tions.

Memory forensics. Oh et  al.  (2024)  presented volGPT ,  a  framework that integrates GPT-3.5-turbo with the Volatility memory forensics tool  to  automate  ransomware  process  triage  in  memory  dumps.  The system  employs  prompt  engineering  techniques  to  guide  the  LLM  in identifying suspicious processes based on process relationships, Virtual Address Descriptor (VAD) information, and malware detection patterns. Experimental evaluation across five ransomware families demonstrated detection  accuracies  ranging  from  87%  to  99%,  with  the  framework successfully  reducing  the  number  of  suspicious  processes  requiring manual  investigation  by  approximately  90%.  However,  the  frame­ work exhibited limitations in handling non-descriptive process names and required manual preprocessing to exclude memory dumps where ransomware processes had already terminated, which emphasises the continued importance of human expertise in complex memory forensic scenarios.

Karne et al. (2025) developed DFIR-Chain , an integrated toolchain combining traditional memory forensics through Volatility 3 , signature-based detection via YARA scanning, and LLM-based summari­ sation  using Mistral-7B served  locally  via Ollama .  The  framework addresses  the  scalability  challenges  of  manual  memory  analysis  by automating the triage of compromised systems through a multi-stage pipeline that produces visual process trees and event timelines. Whilst DFIR-Chain ablation studies showed that removing YARA scanning or LLM summarisation resulted in F1-score reductions of up to 10%, prov­ ing the superior performance of the LLM-assisted approach, the reliance on  signature-based  detection  means  the  framework  may  exhibit  lim­ itations  with  novel  or  polymorphic  malware  variants  not  covered  by existing YARA rules.

Mobile forensics. Thumb ,  a  forensic  automation framework by Shang et  al.  (2025)  leverages  multimodal  LLMs  and  Optical  Character Recognition (OCR) for real-time forensic analysis of physical Android devices.  The  framework integrates Qwen-LV-Plus and ChatGPT-4V to capture on-screen information and simulate user interactions through natural language descriptions, thereby eliminating the need for com­ plex scripting or technical expertise. The system uses OCR techniques to  extract  critical  information  displayed  on  device  screens,  including keywords and category details, allowing for precise and context-aware interactions  with  applications.  As  one  of  its  limitations,  the  frame­ work results in extended execution times due to the requirement for autonomous interpretation of on-screen information and extraction of complete  artefacts.  Additionally,  in-depth  analysis  capabilities  based on file types, including encoding and decoding, as well as encryption and decryption support, require further improvement. The reliance on device rooting and the current limitation to English-language process­ ing present additional practical constraints for broader deployment in diverse digital forensic scenarios.

Cloud forensics. Alharthi and Yasaei (2025) introduced a comprehen­ sive framework for LLM-powered automated cloud forensic investiga­ tions, addressing the scalability challenges posed by the massive volume, velocity, and variety of cloud-generated logs based on few-shot learning techniques to classify log data and reconstruct attack timelines across distributed cloud infrastructure. Comparative evaluation against tradi­ tional machine learning models including Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting demonstrated that LLM-based automation achieved superior forensic accuracy, precision, and recall whilst reducing the need for extensive feature engineering (89.34% accuracy on training data and 86.87% on testing data). However, critical challenges including halluci­ nation risks, adversarial manipulation through log poisoning techniques, and forensic explainability concerns stemming from the black-box na­ ture of LLM decision-making still persist with the authors emphasising the  necessity  for  robust  forensic  validation  mechanisms,  adversarial training, and explainability mechanisms to ensure forensic accuracy in operational cloud environments.

Infotainment  forensics. Fayyaz  et  al.  (2024)  proposed  a  hybrid  AI framework  combining  unsupervised  learning  clustering  with  LLM

analysis for forensic examination of vehicle infotainment systems. The framework integrates K-means clustering to group similar data points with ChatGPT and GPT-4 for contextual analysis of extracted artefacts. Empirical  validation  using  a  set  of  Hyundai  and  Mitsubishi  infotain­ ment system datasets provided improved recall rates, with contact name and phone number extraction improving by 18% and 3% respectively in  the  Hyundai  dataset,  and  song  name  recall  improving  by  2%  in the Mitsubishi dataset. The hybrid approach facilitated the discovery of forensically valuable data including geographical locations and con­ nected  devices  that  would  have  been  infeasible  to  identify  through either manual analysis or clustering alone. However, the study acknowl­ edged the presence of hallucinations in LLM outputs, emphasising the continued necessity for expert human verification of extracted evidence.

Conversational  data  processing. Kahvedžić (2024) presented method­ ologies for handling chat data across multiple LLM architectures, ad­ dressing the unique characteristics of conversational evidence including temporal  sequencing  and  multi-participant  dialogue  analysis.  Their framework demonstrated that structured preprocessing of chat data im­ proved LLM-based analysis accuracy by 23.4% compared to raw text processing. This finding emphasised the importance of domain-specific data preparation in such forensic workflows, as well as considering dif­ ferent models due to varying capabilities in maintaining conversational context and identifying relevant forensic patterns.

Cho et al. (2025) conducted comprehensive forensic analysis of ma­ jor  conversational  AI  platforms  including ChatGPT , Gemini , Copilot , and Claude , presenting methodologies for cross-platform chat log anal­ ysis  and  multi-platform  artefact  correlation.  The  research  provided valuable practical insights into specialised artefact collection method­ ologies  across  different  platforms  and  architectural  approaches,  sup­ ported by concrete case studies demonstrating real-world applicability. The  work  presented  limited  quantitative  evaluation  of  the  proposed methodologies, likely requiring additional validation by practitioners in operational forensic environments.

LLM-integrated  application  forensics. Chernyshev  et  al.  (2024)  intro­ duced a novel approach to digital forensic analysis specifically targeting security incidents in LLM-integrated applications, seeking to address the challenge of investigating prompt injection attacks against systems that incorporate LLMs as core architectural components. Their work proposes LLM invocation logging as a critical component for enhancing digital forensic  readiness  in  LLM-integrated  applications.  The  proposed  LLM invocation  logging  approach  captures  essential  forensic  attributes  in­ cluding timestamps, request identifiers, complete input prompts, and generated outputs in structured JSON format, creating comprehensive audit trails that enable retrospective incident reconstruction. However, the  study  acknowledges  significant  limitations  including  the  reliance on synthetic datasets, and the lack of assessment regarding resilience against anti-forensics techniques, emphasising the need for future re­ search examining LLM performance across diverse attack vectors and real-world deployment contexts.

A2P message forensics. Kim et al. (2025a) introduced SERENA , a frame­ work based on LLM capabilities for systematic extraction and reconstruc­ tion of Application-to-Person (A2P) messages. This framework achieved 98% classification accuracy on ground truth datasets and demonstrated robust performance across diverse service providers and message for­ mats.  The  comprehensive  approach  to  semi-structured  data  analysis addresses a previously underexplored, yet forensically valuable evidence source. The ability of SERENA to extract and normalise forensic entities across heterogeneous A2P message formats without requiring servicespecific parsers represents a significant scalability advantage. Yet, heavy reliance on synthetic datasets and the current evaluation in the context of English-language processing may restrict immediate applicability to diverse real-world contexts.

Knowledge  graph-based  analysis. Zhang  and  Xie  (2024)  developed ForensiQ ,  a  knowledge  graph  question  answering  (KGQA)  system specifically  designed  for  IoT  forensics,  combining BERT and RoBERTa models with graph neural networks to process over 6 million unique entities across 45 million triples. The approach integrates multiple on­ tologies  including  the  Semantic  Sensor  Network  (SSN)  Ontology  and Smart  Applications  REFerence  (SAREF)  ontology  to  create  compre­ hensive forensic knowledge representations and demonstrated superior performance compared to existing KGQA models, with higher accuracy scores across different question complexity levels. However, the reliance on template-based question generation (over 10,000 synthetic questions from over 100 templates) poses challenges associated with the diversity and realism of evaluation scenarios. The dataset construction process, which removed over 99.6% of triples to create a reduced knowledge graph, may also not adequately represent the complexity of real-world forensic investigations.

Analogously, Khatiwala et al. (2025) proposed a structured frame­ work  that  automates  forensic  artefact  extraction  and  refines  data through  LLM-driven  analysis,  with  further  result  validation  using  a Digital  Forensic  Knowledge  Graph  (DFKG).  Based  on  a  13  GB  foren­ sic image dataset containing 61 applications, 2864 databases, and 5870 tables,  the  framework  supports  artefact  traceability  and  evidentiary consistency through deterministic Unique Identifiers (UIDs) and foren­ sic cross-referencing. The methodology achieved over 95% accuracy in artefact extraction, strong support for chain-of-custody adherence, and robust contextual consistency in forensic relationships. The reported re­ sults validate the framework's ability to enhance reliability and establish a legally sound paradigm for AI-assisted digital forensics.

Also,  Zhou  et  al.  (2025)  introduced  Forensic  Intelligence  Graphs (FIGs) as an LLM-driven approach for the automated construction of ev­ idence networks that visually represent relationships between forensic entities extracted from mobile devices. The presented methodology em­ ploys GPT-4o to extract evidence entities from Android device images and infers relationships based on data storage patterns. Similarly, Xu and Deng (2025) developed comprehensive methodologies for knowl­ edge graph reconstruction from mobile device evidence, demonstrating LLM capabilities in extracting entities from SQLite databases and estab­ lishing relationships using the Structured Threat Information Expression (STIX) framework. Their approach compared one-shot versus zero-shot learning techniques in STIX-based entity relationship mapping to pro­ vide empirical validation of different prompting strategies for forensic knowledge graph construction.

Cross-linguistic evidence analysis. For the first time, Kim et al. (2025b) conducted a systematic evaluation of LLM performance in real law en­ forcement contexts using over 140,000 authentic forensic messages from drug-related investigations by the Korean National Police Agency. The comparative analysis of GPT-4o , Gemini 1.5 ,  and Claude 3.5 across precision,  recall,  F1-score,  and  hallucination  rate  metrics,  combined with iterative human-in-the-loop evaluation, represents a key method­ ological advancement in this domain. The study showed that LLMs can effectively process non-English forensic data while maintaining high ac­ curacy levels, with GPT-4o achieving the highest overall performance across  multiple  evaluation  metrics.  However,  the  focus  on  a  single language and crime type may limit broader applicability.

## 4.2.7  .  Cyber incident timeline generation

Unlike traditional timeline tools that provide simple temporal cor­ relations,  the  previously  introduced GenDFIR framework  employs  se­ mantic enrichment to weight the relevance of each event relative to investigative queries, supporting more nuanced identification of causal relationships within incident sequences. The associated two-stage archi­ tecture first transforms incident events into a comprehensive knowledge base  through  data  preprocessing  and  structuring,  and  then  leverages a RAG agent to retrieve relevant events while the LLM processes con­ text  for  detailed  interpretation.  This  approach  achieved  100%  exact match  and  top-k  evidence  retrieval  rates  across  six  diverse  incident scenarios  including  SYN  flood  attacks,  phishing  campaigns,  and  DNS spoofing  incidents,  proving  the  ability  to  maintain  forensic  integrity while automating complex timeline reconstruction tasks. However, the evaluation  relied  exclusively  on  synthetic  cyber  incident  events  in controlled environments.

## 4.2.8  .  Neuro-symbolic validation

The  integration  of  symbolic  AI  (Garnelo  and  Shanahan,  2019) with  neural  approaches  represents  a  significant  advancement  in  ad­ dressing the explainability and validation challenges inherent to LLMassisted forensic analysis. Parsonage and Dempsey (2025) introduced the Automated Generative AI-Driven Forensic Analysis (AGAFA) frame­ work, which combines multimodal LLMs with ontology-based knowl­ edge  representation  and  Answer  Set  Programming  (ASP)  validation rules.  This  neuro-symbolic  architecture  addresses  critical  forensic  re­ quirements through the integration of explicit symbolic reasoning mech­ anisms that  validate  LLM-generated  inferences  against  formal  logical rulesets  and  constraints  that  leverage  domain-specific  ontologies  to capture  forensic  workflow  knowledge.  The  AGAFA  approach  demon­ strates  how  symbolic  AI  can  provide  formal  guarantees  of  behaviour and human-interpretable explanations for forensic conclusions, aligned with ISO 17025 accreditation standards for forensic service providers.

## 4.3  .  Evidence presentation and reporting

This  section  examines  how  LLMs  enhance  evidence  presentation through automated documentation, structured summarisation, natural language interfaces, and standardised evidence representation.

## 4.3.1  .  Report generation

Scanlon et al. (2023a) demonstrated ChatGPT 's general capability to generate well-commented forensic scripts and technical documentation, which still require validation by domain experts. In a more specialised study, Michelet and Breitinger (2024) conducted a comprehensive eval­ uation of both cloud-based ( ChatGPT-3.5 )  and  local  ( Llama-2 )  LLMs for the automated generation of forensic report sections. ChatGPT-3.5 consistently outperformed local Llama-2 models in accuracy, complete­ ness,  and  coherence,  achieving  superior  performance  in  generating structured  report  sections.  However,  both  models  required  extensive proofreading  and  corrections,  with Llama-2 showing  particular  limi­ tations  in  processing  complex  data  formats  and  generating  accurate technical details for relevant sections of the report. The study demon­ strated the potential of LLM-assisted generation for certain structured report sections, yet more complex sections requiring human investigator expertise and expert interpretation remained challenging for LLM-based generation.

## 4.3.2  .  Evidence summarisation

The  ability  to  synthesise  large  volumes  of  forensic  data  into  co­ herent  and  actionable  summaries  represents  a  critical  capability  for digital forensic investigations, with several studies demonstrating how LLMs can transform raw forensic artefacts into structured summaries. LLMs have proven effective at condensing lengthy electronic commu­ nications  such  as  email  threads  and  chat  conversations  into  concise summaries that highlight key forensic elements, extracting salient infor­ mation from unstructured communication data, though the quality of summarisation depends heavily on the model's understanding of the un­ derlying query languages and data structures (Henseler and van Beek, 2023). Combining unsupervised clustering with LLM analysis can also support  more  sophisticated  evidence  summarisation,  where  grouping similar data points through K-means clustering followed by contextual analysis  achieves  improved  recall  rates,  showing  that  LLMs  can  pro­ vide semantic enrichment to clustered forensic data and transform raw artefacts into interpretable summaries (Fayyaz et al., 2024). Similarly, RAG-based  semantically  enriched  timeline  summaries  of  cyber  inci­ dents  combined  with  attention-based  event  correlation  can  support the  creation  of  comprehensive  incident  summaries  that  include  key event  correlations  and  anomaly  identification,  providing  capabilities beyond simple temporal ordering with enriched narratives and causal relationship representations (Loumachi et al., 2025).

## 4.3.3  .  Natural language interfaces

Natural  language  interfaces  represent  a  transformative  capability for  digital  forensic  investigations,  allowing  investigators  to  interact with  forensic  tools  and  data  through  conversational  queries  rather than  command-line  syntax  or  specialised  query  languages,  reducing the  technical  barrier  to  entry  for  forensic  analysis.  LLMs  can  effec­ tively translate natural language investigative questions into structured queries for forensic platforms, successfully generating syntactically cor­ rect  queries  for  common  forensic  scenarios  (Henseler  and  van  Beek, 2023). Additionally, specialised forensic chatbots using RAG-based im­ plementations  have  achieved  91.3%  accuracy  in  providing  forensic guidance across common investigative scenarios, showcasing the poten­ tial  for  LLM-based  decision  support  systems  in  digital  forensics  (Kim et al., 2024).

These  natural  language  interfaces  could  fundamentally  transform how  investigators  interact  with  forensic  tools  and  knowledge  bases, supporting more intuitive and guided exploration and presentation of evidence. As a critical consideration, the studies consistently emphasise that any such interfaces should augment rather than replace human ex­ pertise, particularly in complex or novel investigative scenarios where contextual understanding and domain knowledge remain critical.

## 4.3.4  .  Structured evidence representation

The generation of structured, machine-readable representations of digital evidence enhances interoperability, traceability, and standardi­ sation across diverse investigative tools and workflows. For example, LLM-based frameworks can automate the generation of Digital Forensic Knowledge Graphs (DFKG), achieving over 95% accuracy in artefact extraction while maintaining chain-of-custody adherence through de­ terministic Unique Identifiers (UIDs), successfully processing large-scale forensic datasets to automatically extract and refine forensic artefacts (Khatiwala et al., 2025). The demonstrated ability to maintain 100% chain-of-custody  shows  that  LLM-assisted  approaches  can  be  applied to generate structured evidence representations meeting forensic stan­ dards for traceability and integrity, providing a scalable and auditable methodology for transforming raw forensic data into structured repre­ sentations conducive to both human analysis and automated reasoning systems (Khatiwala et al., 2025).

## 4.3.5  .  Domain-specific forensic documentation

Sharma et al. (2025) introduced ForensicLLM , which is a purposebuilt forensic model developed through the Retrieval-Augmented FineTuning (RAFT) methodology based on the LLaMA-3.1-8B model finetuned on Q&amp;A samples extracted from digital forensic research articles and curated digital artefacts. This LLM achieved superior performance compared to both the base LLaMA-3.1-8B model and the RAG-based model, accurately attributing sources 86.6% of the time, with 81.2% of the responses including both authors and titles. This citation-backed forensic explanation capability addresses a critical gap in LLM-assisted forensic analysis by ensuring that generated insights can be traced back to peer-reviewed literature. Combined with a privacy-preserving local deployment architecture, forensic practitioners can use this approach to handle sensitive case information without exposing data to third-party cloud services.

## 4.4  .  Summary of capabilities

The  systematic  examination  of  LLM-assisted  forensic  capabilities across  the  DFRWS  process  model  demonstrates  iterative  progress  in automating  and  enhancing  traditional  investigative  workflows,  with research  efforts  predominantly  concentrated  in  the  examination  and analysis phases. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the distribution of capabilities across the identified strategic integration points shows clear patterns in how LLMs have been applied to digital forensic challenges.

Pattern recognition capabilities reflect the natural alignment between LLM capabilities in automated detection and the information process­ ing requirements of digital forensic investigations, where the dominance of artefact identification research emphasises the focus on leveraging LLMs to address scalability challenges in processing large volumes of heterogeneous digital evidence.

Evidence analysis capabilities demonstrate the greatest diversity with 15  papers  exploring  specialised  forensic  analysis  across  multiple  do­ mains. As shown in the detailed inset breakdown in Fig. 6, knowledge graph-based analysis leads specialised applications with 4 papers, fol­ lowed by memory forensics and conversational  data  processing  with 2 papers each. Single-paper contributions represent additional digital forensic subdomains such as cloud forensics, mobile forensics, password analysis,  and  infotainment  forensics,  collectively  demonstrating  the breadth of areas where LLMs show promise in specialised analysis tasks. Beyond specialised analysis and other areas such as communication and text analysis, code analysis and generation, and natural language query formulation, other applications in neuro-symbolic validation and entity recognition are also starting to emerge.

Fig. 6. Number of papers mapped to each of the identified large language-model assisted forensic capabilities. Note: papers can map to more than one capability. A dedicated inset is provided for 'Evidence analysis - specialised forensic analysis'.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Finally, evidence presentation and reporting capabilities show substan­ tial  research  attention  with  7  papers  addressing  evidence  summari­ sation,  reflecting  the  critical  need  for  synthesising  large  volumes  of digital forensic data into actionable intelligence and reportable findings. Furthermore,  structured  evidence  representation  receives  significant focus with 6 papers, demonstrating a growing recognition of the impor­ tance of machine-readable forensic outputs that support interoperability and automated reasoning, alongside report generation (5 papers) and natural language interfaces (3 papers).

LLMs have proven effective across these diverse forensic domains, achieving  detection  accuracies  exceeding  85%  in  threat  hunting  sce­ narios  (Lin,  2024),  94.6%  precision  in  log  anomaly  detection  (Pan et al., 2024), and 98% classification accuracy in specialised evidence extraction tasks (Kim et al., 2025a). The recent emergence of purposebuilt forensic models such as ForensicLLM (Sharma et al., 2025) and comprehensive frameworks like GenDFIR (Loumachi et al., 2025) show­ cases the evolution from general-purpose model applications towards domain-specific adaptations. The demonstrated effectiveness for work­ flow  enhancement  across  pattern  recognition,  evidence  analysis,  and evidence presentation tasks positions LLMs as valuable augmentation tools for digital forensic practitioners, provided appropriate validation controls and quality assurance mechanisms are maintained to ensure forensic integrity.

## 4.5  .  Summary of LLMs used

The  systematic  examination  of  LLM  architectures  explored  across the  included  literature,  as  documented  in  Table  2  and  visualised  in Fig.  7,  demonstrates  the  breadth  of  available  models  considered  by researchers. The landscape demonstrates significant dominance of pro­ prietary frontier models, particularly OpenAI's GPT family, collectively followed  by  Anthropic's Claude models and Google's Gemini family. The usage of these commercial models predominantly employs direct prompting, conversational AI interfaces, and RAG-based frameworks to process diverse evidence sources ranging from traditional OS artefacts and network packets to contemporary conversational AI chat logs and multimodal crime scene photographs. The temporal progression shows initial reliance on general-purpose models such as GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 , evolving towards multimodal capabilities through GPT-4o , GPT-4V and specialised fine-tuning approaches by 2025.

At the same time, open-weight alternatives, such as Meta's LLaMA family, demonstrate growing adoption for privacy-preserving local de­ ployment scenarios,  with  applications  concentrated  in  threat-hunting automation, multi-agent frameworks, and domain-specific fine-tuning through RAFT and QLoRA approaches. Furthermore, specialised models represent  an  emerging  trend  towards  purpose-built  forensic  capabili­ ties,  exemplified  by ForensicLLM (Sharma  et  al.,  2025)  for  citationbacked forensic analysis, PassGPT (Imhof et al., 2024) for context-based password  cracking,  and  language-specific  models  such  as EEVE and Polyglot (Cho  et  al.,  2024)  for  cross-linguistic  forensic  analysis  of artefacts in the Korean language.
