![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig. 8. The taxonomy of challenges in LLM-assisted digital forensics.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

The architectural diversity represented in evaluated works also ex­ tends to hybrid and multi-model approaches that combine traditional ML techniques with LLM capabilities, neuro-symbolic frameworks inte­ grating Answer Set Programming validation (Parsonage and Dempsey, 2025), and zero-shot models optimised for anomaly detection in forensic timelines (Agus Ariesta Putra et al., 2024). The inherent heterogeneity represented in model selection and explored architectural approaches reflects  the  ongoing  exploration  efforts  seeking  to  determine  optimal trade-offs between model performance capabilities, as well as practical deployment constraints and data confidentiality requirements.

## 5  .  Challenges and limitations in LLM-assisted digital forensics

The  integration  of  LLMs  into  digital  forensic  workflows,  while demonstrating significant potential across the DFRWS process model as examined in Section 4, introduces a multifaceted set of challenges, as illustrated in Fig. 8.

We organise these challenges into seven conceptually distinct cate­ gories that reflect different perspectives on LLM-assisted digital forensic practice. Technical challenges address fundamental model design con­ straints  and  computational  requirements  that  affect  what  LLMs  can accomplish. Methodological challenges focus on how we evaluate and integrate LLM capabilities into forensic workflows. Forensic integrity con­ cerns centre on maintaining scientific rigour and meeting evidentiary standards. Data  governance  and  security encompasses  privacy,  confi­ dentiality, and deployment security considerations. Operational deploy­ ment challenges address practical implementation barriers in real-world forensic  contexts. Domain-specific limitations  capture  constraints  aris­ ing from particular forensic tasks and digital evidence types. Finally, systemic bias  and  fairness concerns examine how training data biases and  algorithmic  decisions  may  produce  inequitable  outcomes  across different populations. While some challenges manifest across multiple categories, each category represents a distinct analytical lens through which to examine LLM integration challenges in digital forensics.

## 5.1  .  Technical

The technical challenges facing LLM-assisted digital forensics stem from  fundamental  architectural  characteristics  of  transformer-based models that conflict with core forensic requirements.

The  probabilistic  nature  of  LLM  output  generation  presents  fun­ damental challenges for  digital  forensic  applications  requiring  repro­ ducibility  (Scanlon  et  al.,  2023a; Agus  Ariesta  Putra  et  al.,  2024). Scanlon et al. (2023a) demonstrated that identical inputs can produce varying and incorrect outputs across multiple queries, particularly when analysing network packet interpretation. This variability directly con­ flicts with the strict reproducibility requirements essential for the legal admissibility of digital evidence, where forensic methods must produce consistent results when applied to identical inputs under controlled con­ ditions. The magnitude of this challenge extends beyond simple output variation.  Agus  Ariesta  Putra  et  al.  (2024)  observed  significant  per­ formance variability across different zero-shot models, with accuracy differences reaching 21.8% when detecting anomalous log entries. This substantial variation emphasises that model selection itself becomes a critical forensic decision requiring rigorous justification. Furthermore, Khatiwala et al. (2025) demonstrated that whilst LLM-assisted forensic frameworks can achieve high accuracy rates exceeding 95%, the un­ derlying probabilistic generation mechanism introduces variability that must be carefully  managed  through  deterministic  validation  controls and comprehensive audit trails.

Context window constraints fundamentally limit the volume of in­ formation that can be processed simultaneously (Scanlon et al., 2023a). Contemporary LLMs demonstrate substantial performance degradation when processing input sequences beyond their trained context length, exhibiting characteristic U-shaped performance curves where informa­ tion  at  the  beginning  and  end  of  long  contexts  is  processed  more effectively than content in the middle (Liu et al., 2024). This positional attention  bias  presents  significant  challenges  when  analysing  largescale forensic datasets that exceed model capacity constraints, requiring investigators to implement chunking strategies that may fragment con­ textual relationships essential for comprehensive evidence analysis. The scalability  implications  extend  beyond  simple  token  limits.  Forensic investigations frequently involve processing gigabytes of log data, exten­ sive communication histories, or comprehensive system snapshots that far exceed even the largest contemporary context windows. Whilst ap­ proaches such as RAG-Log (Pan et al., 2024) attempt to address these limitations  through  retrieval-augmented  architectures,  such  solutions introduce additional complexity and potential points of failure in the forensic workflow.

LLMs also demonstrate significant limitations in processing binary and non-textual data that constitute substantial portions of digital foren­ sic  evidence  (Scanlon  et  al.,  2023a; Jia  et  al.,  2024).  Scanlon  et  al. (2023a) found that ChatGPT exhibited inconsistent performance when analysing  binary  data  representations,  providing  incorrect  and  nondeterministic interpretations of network packet contents across multiple invocations. Multimodal LLMs, whilst extending capabilities to visual data,  also  face  similar  constraints.  Jia  et  al.  (2024)  highlighted  that vision-capable  models  cannot  incorporate  signal-level  forensic  tech­ niques  or  statistical  cues  for  detecting  AI-generated  content,  limiting their  effectiveness  compared  to  specialised  detection  methodologies based on low-level signal analysis.

Hallucinations represent a critical technical limitation where LLMs generate plausible yet factually incorrect information that can mislead investigations (Scanlon et al., 2023a,b; Sreya et al., 2023; Fayyaz et al., 2024).  Scanlon  et  al.  (2023a)  documented  instances  where ChatGPT generated fabricated tool names and non-existent references with high confidence, demonstrating that the persuasive nature of LLM outputs can mask fundamental inaccuracies. The challenge is compounded by the  difficulty  in  distinguishing  hallucinated  content  from  legitimate information without external verification. Fayyaz et al. (2024) acknowl­ edged the presence of hallucinations in LLM outputs during infotainment system  forensics,  requiring  continuous  expert  human  verification  of all extracted evidence. This requirement for comprehensive validation introduces potential bottlenecks in forensic workflows and raises funda­ mental questions about the practical efficiency gains achievable through LLM integration when human oversight remains mandatory for quality assurance.

Beyond  technical  constraints,  LLMs  exhibit  variable  output  qual­ ity  and  reliability  that  directly  impact  their  digital  forensic  utility. These  quality  challenges  manifest  as  inconsistent  responses,  perfor­ mance degradation  under  adverse  conditions,  and  difficulties  in  sys­ tematic  validation.  Inconsistent  response  quality  across  multiple  in­ vocations  of  identical  queries  contributes  to  fundamental  reliability challenges that complicate forensic validation (Scanlon et al., 2023a; Agus Ariesta Putra et al., 2024; Talasari et al., 2024). Scanlon et al. (2023a) documented instances where ChatGPT provided varying inter­ pretations  of  identical  network  packet  data  across  repeated  queries, demonstrating that output variability extends beyond minor phrasing differences to encompass substantive analytical conclusions. This con­ sistency challenge manifests differently across forensic tasks and data types. Talasari et al. (2024) found that both ChatGPT-3.5 and Claude models failed completely when processing non-descriptive log entries, returning empty strings for messages lacking sufficient contextual in­ formation. This binary failure mode, where models either succeed or provide no output, differs from gradual performance degradation and re­ quires distinct validation approaches to ensure comprehensive evidence processing.

LLM performance degrades substantially under adverse conditions commonly  encountered  in  forensic  contexts,  including  data  corrup­ tion,  compression,  and  adversarial  manipulation.  Jia  et  al.  (2024) observed that multimodal LLM accuracy in detecting AI-generated im­ ages dropped for compressed images, highlighting sensitivity to image quality and post-processing operations that commonly occur in foren­ sic  contexts.  This  performance  degradation  under  realistic  conditions suggests that controlled laboratory validation using pristine data may substantially overestimate operational effectiveness.

The  challenge  extends  to  adversarial  scenarios  where  malicious actors may deliberately manipulate evidence to evade LLM-based detec­ tion or analysis. Whilst some studies acknowledge adversarial robustness concerns  (Jia  et  al.,  2024),  systematic  evaluation  of  LLM  resilience against  adversarial  manipulation  (antiforensics)  in  forensic  contexts remains  limited.  This  gap  is  particularly  concerning  given  the  ad­ versarial nature of criminal investigations, where sophisticated actors may specifically target LLM-based forensic tools to evade detection or undermine evidence integrity.

## 5.2  .  Methodological

The  lack  of  standardised  evaluation  frameworks  for  LLM-assisted forensic tasks prevents meaningful comparison across studies and hin­ ders  cumulative  knowledge  development.  Different  studies  rely  on varying  and  often  incompatible  metrics,  evaluation  criteria,  and  ex­ perimental designs that preclude systematic comparison of alternative approaches. Agus Ariesta Putra et al. (2024) demonstrated this challenge through a comparative analysis of six zero-shot models, revealing per­ formance variations of up to 21.8% across evaluated models, yet lacking standardised benchmarks that would enable systematic comparison with alternative approaches or validation across different forensic contexts. Recent efforts towards standardisation show promise but remain limited in  scope.  Khatiwala et  al.  (2025)  introduced  forensic-specific  metrics including  Evidence  Extraction  Accuracy  (EEA)  and  Forensic  Artefact Precision  (FAP),  representing  progress  towards  domain-appropriate evaluation  criteria.  However,  these  metrics  remain  confined  to  spe­ cific  studies  with  controlled  environments  and  synthetic  data,  lim­ iting  their  generalisability  to  operational  forensic  contexts  where data  complexity  and  adversarial  conditions  may  significantly  impact performance.

The predominant reliance on synthetic datasets across the literature creates a substantial gap between experimental validation and proven operational  applicability  (Loumachi  et  al.,  2025; Kim et  al.,  2025b). Whilst  Loumachi et al.  (2025)  achieved  97.51%  overall  performance on  synthetic  cyber  incident  events,  the  authors  explicitly  acknowl­ edge that the evaluation relied exclusively on controlled environments, raising  questions  about  performance  degradation  in  real-world  sce­ narios  characterised  by  incomplete  data  and  unexpected  edge  cases. Kim et al. (2025b) conducted the first systematic evaluation using au­ thentic law enforcement data, demonstrating that LLM performance in operational forensic contexts may differ substantially from controlled laboratory settings.  This  pioneering  work  highlights  the  critical  need for validation using real-world forensic data that captures the complex­ ity,  ambiguity, and adversarial nature of actual investigations, rather than  sanitised  synthetic  scenarios  that  may  not  adequately  represent operational challenges.

General-purpose LLMs face significant challenges when applied to highly  specialised  forensic  contexts  requiring  domain-specific  knowl­ edge and terminology (Henseler and van Beek, 2023; Cho et al., 2024). Henseler and van Beek (2023) identified substantial limitations when models  lack  contextual  understanding  of  specific  organisational  pro­ cesses  or  proprietary  data  formats,  emphasising  the  importance  of domain-specific  training  requirements  for  effective  forensic  applica­ tions.  The  challenge  extends  beyond  simple  vocabulary  adaptation to  encompass  understanding  of  complex  forensic  methodologies,  le­ gal  requirements,  and  investigative  workflows  that  may  not  be  wellrepresented in general training corpora.

## 5.3  .  Forensic integrity

The black-box nature of LLM decision-making processes presents fun­ damental challenges for maintaining forensic integrity and meeting legal admissibility standards (Scanlon et al., 2023a,b). Scanlon et al. (2023b) emphasised that the inability to explain the precise processes followed in identifying evidence may render such evidence inadmissible under established legal frameworks. The complex architecture of transformerbased  models  means  that  even  with  detailed  prompting  requesting step-by-step  explanations,  the  underlying  reasoning  process  remains opaque to investigators, potentially failing to meet transparency require­ ments for scientific evidence admissibility. This explainability challenge extends beyond simple output justification to encompass the entire ana­ lytical pipeline. Forensic practitioners must be able to articulate not only what conclusions  were  reached,  but  precisely  how  those  conclusions were derived, what alternative hypotheses were considered, and why specific analytical approaches were selected. The probabilistic nature of LLM generation, combined with the extensive parameter spaces of con­ temporary models, makes such comprehensive explanation extremely difficult if not practically impossible with current architectures.

The reliability of LLM-assisted forensic analysis varies significantly based  on  scenario  complexity,  novelty,  and  the  degree  to  which  in­ vestigative  contexts  align  with  training  data  distributions.  Kim  et  al. (2024) demonstrated that whilst their forensic chatbot achieved 91.3% accuracy in providing guidance across common investigative scenarios, performance degraded substantially to 74.2% when handling novel or edge-case scenarios not well-represented in training data. This perfor­ mance degradation in unfamiliar contexts presents critical challenges for forensic applications, where investigators frequently encounter unique circumstances  requiring  adaptation  beyond  established  patterns.  The consistency challenge is compounded by the non-deterministic nature of LLM outputs discussed previously. Forensic methods must produce re­ liable and reproducible results across multiple applications to identical inputs, yet LLM architectures inherently introduce variability through probabilistic sampling mechanisms. And whilst approaches such as tem­ perature reduction can mitigate some variability to an extent, complete determinism remains unachievable, creating fundamental tension with forensic requirements for reproducibility.

Maintaining a comprehensive chain of custody in LLM-assisted in­ vestigations also requires specialised approaches that extend traditional evidence-handling  protocols  to  encompass  AI-assisted  analytical  pro­ cesses.  Khatiwala  et  al.  (2025)  demonstrated  that  achieving  100% chain-of-custody  adherence  requires  deterministic  Unique  Identifiers (UIDs) and comprehensive cross-referencing mechanisms that track not only evidence artefacts but also the specific LLM invocations, prompts, parameters,  and  outputs  that  contributed  to  analytical  conclusions. However,  these  approaches  require  further  validation  in  operational forensic contexts beyond controlled experimental settings. The challenge extends to documenting model versions, training data provenance, finetuning procedures, and deployment configurations that may influence such derived analytical outcomes.

The continued necessity for human oversight and validation remains critical despite demonstrated LLM capabilities (Scanlon et al., 2023a; Fayyaz et al., 2024). Fayyaz et al. (2024) explicitly acknowledged that despite improved recall rates in infotainment system forensics, the pres­ ence of hallucinations requires expert human verification of all extracted evidence. This requirement for continuous human validation introduces potential bottlenecks in forensic workflows and raises questions about the practical efficiency gains achievable through LLM integration when comprehensive human oversight remains mandatory.

## 5.4  .  Data governance and security

The  reliance  on  cloud-based  LLM  services  introduces  substantial data privacy and confidentiality risks for forensic investigations involv­ ing  sensitive  or  classified  information  (Scanlon  et  al.,  2023a; Sreya et al., 2023; Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024). Sreya et al. (2023) highlighted  that  employing  third-party  AI  models  for  analysis  raises fundamental questions regarding data privacy and confidentiality, as digital forensics frequently involves sensitive and private data requiring strict handling protocols that may be incompatible with cloud service terms of service. The challenge extends beyond simple data exposure to encompass questions of data retention, secondary use, and jurisdic­ tional control. When forensic evidence is processed through cloud-based LLM services, investigators may lose control over data storage locations, retention  periods,  and  potential  secondary  uses  by  service  providers. Wickramasekara and Scanlon (2024) emphasised that these concerns ne­ cessitate careful consideration of local versus cloud-based deployment options, with local deployment potentially addressing privacy concerns at the cost of increased computational requirements and reduced access to state-of-the-art frontier models.

Local  deployment  of  open-weight  LLMs  offers  potential  solutions to data privacy concerns, but introduces significant operational tradeoffs.  Sharma  et  al.  (2025)  demonstrated  that  purpose-built  forensic LLMs such as ForensicLLM can be deployed locally to handle sensitive case information without exposing data to third-party cloud services. However, local deployment requires substantial computational infras­ tructure, ongoing model maintenance, and technical expertise that may exceed existing capabilities. The trade-offs extend to model performance and capability. Locally deployable models typically lag behind frontier cloud-based models in capabilities due to computational constraints and limited access to training resources.

## 5.5  .  Operational deployment

Effective  prompt  engineering  represents  a  significant  operational challenge  requiring  specialised  skills  that  may  not  be  present  in  tra­ ditional forensic training (Scanlon et al., 2023a; Jia et al., 2024). Jia et al. (2024) demonstrated that simple binary prompts proved ineffec­ tive for multimodal forensic analysis, with LLMs frequently declining to  respond  due  to  a  lack  of  context  or  safety  guardrail  activation. More sophisticated context-rich prompts achieved substantially lower rejection rates, but required specialised prompt engineering expertise that  may  not  be  readily  available  in  traditional  forensic  skill  sets. This  skill  gap  necessitates  substantial  training  investment  to  develop prompt engineering capabilities among forensic practitioners. The chal­ lenge is  compounded by the rapid evolution of LLM capabilities and best practices, requiring continuous professional development to main­ tain effectiveness. Furthermore, optimal prompting strategies may vary significantly  across  different  models  and  forensic  contexts,  requiring practitioners to develop adaptive expertise rather than memorising fixed prompt templates.

The computational demands of LLM-assisted forensic analysis vary substantially based on deployment approach and model selection (Imhof et al., 2024; Oh et al., 2024). Imhof et al. (2024) documented that model training times ranged from approximately 40 min to over 32 h depend­ ing on training data size, with training time directly correlated to dataset volume. These computational demands raise questions about trade-offs between larger training datasets and faster model creation times in timesensitive forensic investigations where rapid response may be critical. Inference performance also varies significantly across deployment ap­ proaches. Oh et al. (2024) documented that text generation using local LLM models required approximately 30 min per analysis, with average durations of 10 s for cloud-based ChatGPT versus 3 min for local Llama models. These performance disparities directly impact the practical de­ ployment of LLM-assisted forensic tools in time-sensitive investigative scenarios, potentially creating bottlenecks that negate efficiency gains from automation.

Recent implementations demonstrate varying operational efficiency trade-offs  that  depend  heavily  on  specific  forensic  tasks  and  imple­ mentation approaches (Hawa et al., 2025; Shang et al., 2025). Hawa et  al.  (2025)  showed  that  multimodal  LLM  analysis  reduced  average analysis  time  by  43%  while  maintaining  88%  accuracy  compared  to expert human analysis, demonstrating substantial efficiency gains for visual  evidence  processing.  However,  Shang  et  al.  (2025)  found  that automated forensic frameworks requiring autonomous interpretation of on-screen information resulted in extended execution times due to se­ quential  processing  requirements  and  action  execution  delays.  These findings suggest that operational efficiency gains may be highly taskdependent, with some forensic applications benefiting substantially from LLM integration, while others may experience performance degradation.

## 5.6  .  Domain-specific

Knowledge cutoff date limitations constrain LLM effectiveness in ad­ dressing  recent  and  constantly  evolving  attack  vectors  (Sreya  et  al., 2023; Scanlon et al., 2023a). Sreya et al. (2023) noted that LLM un­ derstanding is restricted to information available at the time of the most recent training data update, preventing awareness of recent occurrences, changes, or emerging threats, without access to additional tools such as web search. These temporal limitations prove particularly problematic in dynamic threat environments where forensic investigators must anal­ yse  novel  or  zero-day  attack  patterns  and  emerging  technologies  not represented in historical training data.

LLM proficiency in specialised forensic domains also depends heavily on understanding both technical systems and domain-specific investiga­ tive methodologies (Henseler and van Beek, 2023; Cho et al., 2024). Henseler and van Beek (2023) demonstrated that LLM performance de­ grades substantially when models lack contextual understanding of spe­ cific organisational processes, proprietary data formats, or specialised forensic  platforms.  This  limitation  reflects  the  reality  that  forensic investigations  frequently  involve  unique  technical  environments  and customised systems that may not be well-represented in general training corpora.

Demographic and linguistic specialisation presents additional chal­ lenges. Cho et al. (2024) showed that Korean-language LLMs struggled to  distinguish  between adjacent age groups in author profiling tasks, particularly for the 30s, 40s, and 50s age ranges, possibly due to subtle differences in writing styles that current LLM architectures cannot ad­ equately capture. This finding highlights how domain-specific nuances may exceed current LLM capabilities, requiring specialised training or alternative analytical approaches.

Effective  LLM-assisted  forensic  analysis  frequently  requires  sub­ stantial  data  preprocessing  that  introduces  technical  complexity  and potential error sources. Kahvedžić (2024) demonstrated that structured preprocessing of chat data improved LLM-based analysis accuracy by 23.4% compared to raw text processing, emphasising the critical im­ portance  of  domain-specific  data  preparation  in  forensic  workflows. However, this preprocessing overhead adds additional steps to the in­ vestigative  process  and  requires  specialised  knowledge  of  both  the data  format  and  LLM  input  requirements.  The  preprocessing  chal­ lenge is compounded by the diversity of data formats encountered in forensic investigations. Different evidence sources may require distinct preprocessing  approaches,  creating  complexity  in  developing  gener­ alised LLM-assisted forensic tools. Furthermore, preprocessing decisions may significantly impact analytical outcomes, requiring careful docu­ mentation  and  validation  to  maintain  forensic  integrity  and  support reproducibility requirements.

## 5.7  .  Bias and fairness

Systemic bias and fairness concerns in LLM-assisted digital foren­ sics extend beyond technical accuracy issues to encompass fundamental questions  of  equitable  treatment  and  algorithmic  justice.  These  chal­ lenges  manifest  across  training  data  composition  and  demographic performance disparities that may stem from such composition, as well as accountability frameworks.

LLMs learn patterns from vast Internet corpora that inherently reflect societal biases and may contain inaccuracies which can propagate into forensic analysis (Scanlon et al., 2023a; Jia et al., 2024). The challenge is compounded by the opacity of training data composition for many commercial LLMs, making it difficult for forensic practitioners to assess potential bias sources or their impacts on specific investigative contexts. Cho et al. (2024) demonstrated that language-specific model training significantly impacts forensic analysis performance, with accuracy de­ grading to 58.7% when processing mixed-language content. This finding highlights how LLMs may exhibit differential performance across demo­ graphic groups, potentially introducing systematic biases into forensic investigations that disproportionately affect certain populations. Such demographic biases raise fundamental fairness concerns, as investigative outcomes may vary based on the linguistic or cultural characteristics of evidence rather than its substantive content.

The  ethical  framework  surrounding  LLM  use  in  forensics  encom­ passes  transparency  and  fairness  considerations  that  extend  beyond technical performance metrics (Scanlon et al., 2023b; Wickramasekara and Scanlon, 2024). Scanlon et al. (2023b) raised essential questions about the appropriate level of investigator reliance on LLM outputs and the mechanisms needed to ensure fair and non-maleficent application of  these  technologies.  The  black-box  nature  of  LLM  decision-making processes presents particular challenges for maintaining transparency in forensic investigations, where the ability to explain analytical meth­ ods is crucial for both legal proceedings and public accountability. When automated systems contribute to investigative decisions that may affect legal outcomes, determining accountability for errors becomes complex. Should responsibility rest with the forensic practitioner who employed the  LLM,  the  organisation  that  deployed  the  system,  or  the  model provider whose training decisions influenced outputs? Wickramasekara and Scanlon (2024) acknowledged risks including information accuracy and legal barriers in their proposed multi-agent framework; however comprehensive governance frameworks addressing these accountability questions remain underdeveloped.

The  informal  nature  of  chat  data  and  conversational  evidence presents  additional  ethical  challenges  related  to  interpretation  and potential mischaracterisation (Kahvedžić, 2024). LLMs trained on ca­ sual  communication  patterns  may  not  adequately  handle  the  nu­ anced  requirements  of  forensic  contexts  where  informal  language, slang, sarcasm, and cultural references require careful interpretation. Misinterpretation  of  informal  communication  could  lead  to  incorrect conclusions that unfairly characterise individuals or misrepresent their intentions,  raising  concerns  about  the  fairness  and  accuracy  of  LLMassisted analysis in contexts involving everyday digital communications.

The absence of established ethical guidelines for LLM use in forensic contexts creates uncertainty for practitioners and organisations seeking to  deploy  these  technologies  responsibly.  Whilst  technical  capabili­ ties  continue  to  advance,  the  development  of  corresponding  ethical frameworks, professional standards, and governance mechanisms lags substantially  behind,  creating  a  gap  that  may  expose  both  forensic organisations and investigated individuals to potential harm from in­ appropriate or inadequately validated LLM applications.

## 5.8  .  Summary of challenges and limitations

These multifaceted challenges collectively demonstrate that whilst LLMs offer significant potential for enhancing digital forensic capabil­ ities,  substantial  research  and  development  efforts  remain  necessary before these technologies can achieve widespread operational deploy­ ment  whilst  maintaining  the  scientific  rigour  and  legal  defensibility critical  to  digital  forensic  science.  The  path  forward  requires  coor­ dinated  efforts  across  several  domains,  with  particular  emphasis  on developing forensic-specific solutions, rather than attempting to adapt general-purpose LLMs to forensic requirements through basic prompt engineering alone.

## 6  .  Future research directions

The comprehensive analysis of challenges and limitations presented in  Section  5  establishes  a  foundation  for  identifying  critical  research priorities for the advancement of LLM-assisted digital forensics towards operational maturity. As illustrated in Table 3, these research directions span  several  domains,  with  each  addressing  multiple  challenge  cate­ gories identified throughout this survey. These priorities build upon the three strategic integration points established in Section 4, focusing on advancing LLM capabilities in pattern recognition, evidence analysis, and evidence presentation whilst addressing the fundamental challenges that currently constrain practical operational deployments.

## 6.1  .  Technical research needs

## 6.1.1  .  Forensic-ready LLM architectures

The development of forensic-ready LLM architectures requires fun­ damental architectural innovations that embed forensic requirements directly into model design, rather than attempting to retrofit generalpurpose  models  for  forensic  applications.  Built-in  logging  and  audit capabilities must capture comprehensive traces of model invocations, in­ cluding input prompts, generated outputs, intermediate reasoning steps, and all relevant parameters, creating immutable audit trails that support chain-of-custody  requirements  and  allow  for  retrospective  validation of  LLM-assisted  analytical  processes.  Minimally  probabilistic  opera­ tion modes, specifically designed for forensic contexts should support reproducible outputs across multiple invocations of identical inputs, po­ tentially through controlled sampling mechanisms or temperature-zero configurations that minimise probabilistic variation. Explainability-bydesign  approaches  must  move  beyond  post-hoc  interpretation  tech­ niques to incorporate transparent reasoning mechanisms that generate human-interpretable justifications for analytical conclusions, addressing the black-box nature of current LLM architectures that fundamentally conflicts with forensic transparency requirements.

## 6.1.2  .  Advanced analysis techniques

Advanced analysis techniques must address the reliability, quality, and robustness challenges inherent to single-model LLM deployments through  systematic  integration  of  complementary  approaches.  Multimodel ensemble architectures and multi-agent orchestration workflows that  combine outputs from diverse LLM architectures can potentially mitigate  individual  model  limitations  by  reducing  hallucination  risks and improving output quality through various consensus mechanisms, though such approaches require careful validation to ensure that ensem­ ble predictions maintain forensic integrity and do not simply amplify shared biases across the individual models. Uncertainty quantification methods represent another potential critical research priority, enabling LLMs to provide calibrated  confidence  estimates  alongside  analytical outputs, so that forensic practitioners can prioritise human verification efforts based on model uncertainty. Furthermore, adversarial robustness improvements  must  address  the  susceptibility  of  LLMs  to  manipu­ lation  through  carefully  crafted  inputs  via  prompt  injection  attacks, particularly relevant given the adversarial nature of criminal investiga­ tions where sophisticated actors may employ sophisticated anti-forensic techniques.

## 6.1.3  .  Specialised forensic LLMs

The development of specialised forensic LLMs represents a critical research direction that moves beyond general-purpose model adapta­ tion towards purpose-built architectures and knowledge sets optimised for  forensic  requirements.  Domain-specific  fine-tuning  methodologies must extend beyond simple instruction tuning to incorporate forensicspecific  reasoning  patterns,  legal  reasoning  rules  and  terminology, and investigative workflows, building upon foundational work such as ForensicLLM (Sharma et al., 2025). These specialised models should demonstrate improved output quality and reduced hallucination rates compared to general-purpose alternatives when handling digital foren­ sic artefacts. Furthermore, privacy-preserving local deployment archi­ tectures  require  investigation  into  efficient  model  compression  tech­ niques, quantisation strategies, and hardware acceleration approaches that enable the deployment of capable forensic LLMs within resourceconstrained  environments  whilst  maintaining  data  confidentiality  re­ quirements and acceptable performance levels. Also, forensic-optimised prompt engineering frameworks should codify best practices for elic­ iting  reliable  and  high-quality  analytical  outputs  from  LLMs  across diverse  forensic  contexts  and  tasks,  potentially  incorporating  struc­ tured  prompt  templates  and  validation  checkpoints  to  reduce  sensi­ tivity  to  prompt  variations  and  improve  consistency  across  different scenarios.

## 6.2  .  Methodological research needs

## 6.2.1  .  Validation frameworks

Standardised evaluation protocols must establish consistent method­ ologies  for  assessing  LLM  performance  across  diverse  digital  forensic contexts, addressing the current fragmentation where different studies employ  a  wide  variety  of  metrics  and  evaluation  criteria  that  pre­ vent  meaningful  cross-study  comparisons.  The  associated  benchmark dataset development requires the creation of comprehensive, publicly available forensic datasets that capture the complexity and diversity of real-world investigations. Forensic-specific performance metrics could extend beyond traditional accuracy measures to encompass reliability, consistency, explainability, and legal admissibility considerations, build­ ing upon initial efforts such as Evidence Extraction Accuracy (EEA) and Forensic Artefact Precision (FAP) (Khatiwala et al., 2025) to develop comprehensive evaluation frameworks that align with forensic integrity requirements. Reliability assessment methodologies should provide sys­ tematic  approaches  for  quantifying  LLM  consistency  across  multiple invocations, thereby evaluating performance degradation under adverse conditions empirically, and establishing confidence bounds that support risk-based validation strategies in operational forensic contexts.

## 6.2.2  .  Process model refinement

Empirical validation of the proposed DFRWS extensions must move beyond theoretical frameworks to demonstrate practical effectiveness and  real  utility  through  controlled  experiments  and  real-world  de­ ployment  studies,  addressing  the  current  gap  where  comprehensive integration frameworks remain largely unvalidated in operational foren­ sic contexts. Best practice guidelines development requires systematic codification of lessons learned from early LLM deployments in forensic contexts, establishing evidence-based recommendations for LLM and pa­ rameter selection, prompt engineering, validation protocols, and human oversight mechanisms that balance automation benefits with forensic integrity  requirements.  Quality  assurance  protocols  must  also  define systematic approaches for validating LLM outputs to establish appropri­ ate thresholds for human verification, and documenting LLM-assisted forensic processes in ways that support legal admissibility and scientific reproducibility.  These  protocols  should  explicitly  address  the  techni­ cal quality challenges of hallucination detection and output consistency verification.

## 6.2.3  .  Integration strategies

Hybrid human-AI workflow design must establish the optimal divi­ sion of labour between LLM capabilities and human expertise, enumerat­ ing specific forensic tasks where automation and AI-based enhancement provide measurable benefits whilst maintaining required quality stan­ dards. The development of the associated tool ecosystem requires the creation of interoperable forensic platforms that can incorporate LLM ca­ pabilities alongside traditional forensic tools to create combined unified workflows with built-in quality assurance mechanisms. Training curricu­ lum design must address the skill gaps identified across the literature, developing  educational  programmes  that  equip  forensic  practitioners with LLM knowledge and prompt engineering expertise, LLM validation capabilities  including  output  quality  assessment,  and  critical  evalua­ tion skills necessary for the effective deployment of AI-assisted forensic workflows. Finally, organisational adoption roadmaps should provide structured guidance covering technical infrastructure requirements, pol­ icy development and training needs, and phased deployment strategies to manage risks as part of incremental capability development whilst establishing quality benchmarks at each deployment phase.

## 6.3  .  Legal and policy research needs

## 6.3.1  .  Legal framework development

Admissibility  standards  for  LLM-assisted  evidence  must  be  estab­ lished through systematic engagement with legal communities, address­ ing how AI-generated analytical outputs can meet established criteria such as the Daubert standard. Liability and accountability frameworks require clarification of responsibility allocation when LLM-assisted anal­ ysis  contributes  to  investigative  decisions  and  defining  appropriate standards  of  care  for  AI-assisted  forensic  practice.  Cross-jurisdiction harmonisation  efforts  must  address  the  reality  that  digital  investiga­ tions  frequently  span  multiple  legal  systems  with  varying  standards for  evidence  admissibility,  data  privacy  requirements,  and  AI  gover­ nance  frameworks,  requiring  international  coordination  to  establish consistent  approaches  that  support  effective  cross-border  investiga­ tions.  Finally,  regulatory  guidance  development  should  provide  clear direction for forensic organisations regarding appropriate LLM deploy­ ment, validation requirements, documentation standards, and oversight mechanisms, reducing uncertainty that currently constrains operational adoption of these technologies in real-life investigative contexts.

## 6.3.2  .  Ethical guidelines

Bias  detection  and  mitigation  strategies  must  address  the  reality that LLMs trained on Internet corpora inherently reflect societal biases that  can  propagate  into  forensic  analysis.  These  strategies  operate  at two levels - first, addressing technical accuracy degradation and output quality issues caused by biased training data (as discussed in the tech­ nical challenges of Section 5), and second, ensuring systemic fairness by identifying demographic disparities in model performance and im­ plementing corrective measures that ensure equitable treatment across diverse  populations.  This  dual  approach  requires  systematic  method­ ologies for both technical validation of output quality across different input  types  and  fairness  assessment  of  investigative  outcomes  across demographic groups, linguistic communities, and cultural contexts.

Fairness assessment frameworks should establish methodologies for evaluating whether LLM-assisted forensic analysis produces equitable outcomes  across  different  demographic  groups,  addressing  concerns that automated systems may systematically disadvantage certain pop­ ulations through either technical quality degradation or inherent sys­ temic bias. The associated transparency requirements must define ap­ propriate  levels  of  disclosure  regarding  LLM  deployment  in  forensic investigations.

## 6.4  .  Cross-cutting research priorities

Interdisciplinary  collaboration  represents  a  fundamental  require­ ment  for  advancing  LLM-assisted  digital  forensics,  necessitating  sus­ tained partnerships between AI researchers, forensic practitioners, and legal  scholars  that  bridge  disciplinary  boundaries.  Relevant  indus­ try-academia  knowledge  transfer  mechanisms  can  facilitate  bidirec­ tional exchange of insights, with academic researchers gaining access to real-world forensic challenges and operational constraints, while prac­ titioners benefit from cutting-edge technical developments and rigorous scientific evaluation methodologies.

Empirical validation through real-world deployment studies remains critically important, moving beyond controlled laboratory experiments to assess LLM performance in operational forensic environments where data  heterogeneity  and  adversarial  conditions  may  significantly  im­ pact  effectiveness.  Longitudinal  performance  assessment  could  track LLM  capabilities  over  extended  periods,  evaluating  how  model  up­ dates and evolving deployment approaches affect forensic utility over time. Comparative effectiveness research must therefore systematically evaluate alternative LLM architectures, deployment strategies, and inte­ gration approaches across diverse digital forensic contexts, establishing evidence-based  guidance  for  practitioners  regarding  optimal  technol­ ogy selection and implementation approaches that balance operational performance, cost, privacy, and forensic integrity requirements.

## 7  .  Discussion

## 7.1  .  Key findings summary

The presented systematic review describes the rapid progression of LLM integration in digital forensics from exploratory investigations in 2023  to  empirical  validation  efforts  in  2025,  with  research  predom­ inantly  concentrated  in  the  examination  and  analysis  phases  of  the DFRWS  process  model.  The  literature  establishes  that  LLMs  achieve substantial  performance  improvements  across  diverse  forensic  tasks, including detection accuracies exceeding 85% in threat hunting (Lin, 2024),  94.6%  precision  in  log  anomaly  detection  (Pan  et  al.,  2024), and 98% classification accuracy in specialised evidence extraction (Kim et al., 2025a). However, critical challenges persist across a number of key  areas.  The  fundamental  misalignment  between  the  probabilistic nature  of  LLM  architectures  and  the  deterministic  requirements  of forensic science remains the central challenge constraining operational deployment.

## 7.2  .  Implications for practice

Forensic  practitioners  should  approach  LLM  integration  as  an augmentation  strategy  for  human  expertise,  prioritising  applications in  evidence  discovery,  pattern  recognition,  and  preliminary  analysis where  LLM  capabilities  align  with  investigative  requirements  com­ bined with human oversight. Technical approach selection must balance performance  capabilities  against  deployment  constraints,  with  cloudbased  frontier  models  offering  superior  capabilities  at  the  cost  of data  privacy  concerns,  whereas  local  open-weight  alternatives  such as ForensicLLM (Sharma et al., 2025) provide privacy-preserving op­ tions with potentially reduced performance and increased infrastructure requirements. Alongside practical deployment, the associated risk mit­ igation strategies should incorporate comprehensive documentation of LLM invocations, parameters, and outputs to support chain-of-custody requirements, and establish clear governance frameworks defining ap­ propriate use cases, oversight mechanisms, and accountability structures for LLM-assisted forensic analysis.

## 7.3  .  Implications for research

This survey establishes a preliminary framework for systematically evaluating  LLM  integration  in  digital  forensics  through  two  comple­ mentary dimensions. The capability dimension, mapped to the DFRWS process  model  phases,  provides  a  structured  approach  for  assessing where  LLMs  can  provide  measurable  benefits  across  digital  forensic workflows. As illustrated in Fig. 5, this dimension identifies three strate­ gic integration points - (1) pattern recognition during examination, (2) evidence analysis, and (3) evidence presentation. The challenge dimen­ sion,  presented  in  Fig.  8  and  detailed  in  Section  5,  establishes  seven analytical  lenses  through  which  researchers  can  examine  constraints and limitations in LLM-assisted digital forensics. Together, these dimen­ sions provide a systematic framework for evaluating future LLM-assisted forensic approaches.

Priority  research  areas  presented  in  Section  6  must  address  the fundamental challenges of explainability, reproducibility, and legal ad­ missibility through the development of forensic-ready LLM architectures that embed audit capabilities, transparent reasoning mechanisms, and predictable operation modes directly into model design rather than at­ tempting to retrofit general-purpose models for forensic applications. Interdisciplinary collaboration between AI researchers, forensic practi­ tioners, and legal scholars represents a critical enabler for advancing this field, requiring sustained partnerships that facilitate bidirectional knowledge transfer and the establishment of standardised evaluation frameworks. The long-term research agenda must prioritise empirical validation using real-world forensic data instead of extensive reliance on  synthetic  datasets,  combined  with  a  systematic  investigation  of adversarial robustness against anti-forensic techniques.

## 7.4  .  Limitations of current survey

This survey focuses specifically and exclusively on LLM applications in digital forensics, excluding broader AI techniques such as computer vision,  traditional  ML,  and  specialised  neural  architectures  that  also contribute to forensic capabilities, thereby limiting the scope to natu­ ral language processing and reasoning capabilities inherent to LLMs at the time of writing. The rapidly evolving landscape of LLM capabilities means that findings may become outdated as new models, architectures, and  deployment  approaches  emerge,  with  the  literature  review  cap­ turing works published through October 2025 and potentially missing subsequent developments in this highly dynamic field.

## 8  .  Conclusion

We presented a comprehensive examination of the emerging inter­ section  between  generative  AI  and  digital  forensics,  synthesising  33 peer-reviewed works mapped across the phases of the DFRWS process model.  Our  analysis  demonstrates  that  LLMs  can  achieve  substantial performance improvements across diverse forensic tasks, yet their in­ herently  probabilistic  nature  is  not  congruent  with  the  deterministic requirements of digital forensic science. The path forward requires coor­ dinated interdisciplinary research bridging AI, digital forensics, and law to devise forensic-ready LLM architectures that embed explainability, reproducibility,  and  legal  admissibility  requirements,  alongside  stan­ dardised validation frameworks and comprehensive governance mech­ anisms. As the field progresses from exploratory investigations towards operational  maturity  across  specialised  forensic  tasks,  the  researcher and  practitioner  community  must  prioritise  human-AI  collaborative approaches  that  can  leverage  LLM  capabilities  for  pattern  recogni­ tion, preliminary analysis and evidence presentation alongside rigorous human oversight and strict validation controls.

## CRediT authorship contribution statement

Maxim  Chernyshev: Writing -original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zubair Baig: Writing -review &amp; editing, Supervision,  Conceptualization. Naeem  Syed: Writing  -  review  &amp; editing. Robin  Doss: Writing  -  review  &amp;  editing. Malcolm  Shore: Writing - review &amp; editing.
